title: Wall Corner Pie-Cut Kitchen Cabinet
summary: A 30"-tall wall corner cabinet with a pie-cut door pair, sized to hang alongside standard wall cabinets and finished with a real hardwood face frame.
description:
A corner wall cabinet earns its keep by not wasting the corner — this one uses a pie-cut door pair, hinged to each other as well as to the cabinet, so both doors swing open together and give you full access to a space that would otherwise be an awkward reach-in. The face frame is built as its own separate assembly, with its pocket holes hidden on the back side, then attached to the carcass through the same holes you predrilled earlier — building it separately keeps the joinery clean and lets you dial in the door reveals before anything is hung.

Build the face frame and doors from a hardwood species that matches your other cabinets — this is the one piece a mismatched wood grain would be most obvious on, sitting right at eye level in the corner of the room. The hanging cleats carry real cabinet weight, so if you're loading this with heavy dishware, add cleats under the other fixed shelves too, not just the two called for here.

tools.note: Rips the plywood sheet into the two side-width strips.
Cuts the 45° bevel on the hanging cleats.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, ripped into a 22-3/4" strip and an 11-3/4" strip, 8 ft each
materials.note: The leftover width from the 11-3/4" strip is enough for another wall cabinet if you're building more than one.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood, half sheet, for the backs
Concealed euro-style hinges, 1-1/4" overlay face frame
Pie-cut hinges, for joining the two doors
Screws, for hanging and attaching to neighboring cabinets

cutList.note: Longest-point measurement; one end cut at a 45° bevel. Add more under the other fixed shelves for heavier loads.

steps.title: Cut the shelves and sides
steps.body: Cut all your shelves and sides as shown in your reference diagram from the two ripped plywood strips.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into a 22-3/4" strip and an 11-3/4" strip, 8 ft each

steps.title: Drill pocket holes and assemble the carcass
steps.body: Drill pocket holes in all the shelves and sides as shown in your diagram, then attach the shelves to the sides.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped into a 22-3/4" strip and an 11-3/4" strip, 8 ft each

steps.title: Attach the back support cleat
steps.body: Attach the 1x3 back cleat to the back of the cabinet, keeping the shelves level. Countersink the screws and use glue.

steps.title: Attach the hanging cleats
steps.body: These cleats are for hanging the cabinet later. For heavier loads, add more cleats under the remaining fixed shelves rather than relying on just these two. Attach with glue and screws — 3/4" pocket holes on the back side work well here.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame as its own assembly, with hidden 3/4" pocket holes on the back side. Attach it to the carcass using the pocket holes you predrilled in step 2.

steps.title: Size and hang the doors
steps.body: For standard full-overlay doors with a 1/2" reveal between doors, size your doors as shown in your reference. When hanging, adjust so 1/4" of the face frame shows on the ends and 1/2" shows on the top and bottom.

steps.title: Join the two doors with pie-cut hinges
steps.body: Use the pie-cut hinges to join the two doors together so they swing open as a pair.
materials: Pie-cut hinges, for joining the two doors

steps.title: Install the concealed hinges
steps.body: Install the concealed euro-style hinges to hang the door pair on the cabinet. A 1-1/4" overlay face-frame concealed hinge is the right choice for this face frame.
materials: Concealed euro-style hinges, 1-1/4" overlay face frame

images.alt: Wall corner kitchen cabinet with a pie-cut door pair
